"**Poland Is Not Yet Lost**",[[a]](#cite_note-1) known in [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") as "**Mazurek Dąbrowskiego**" (pronounced [[maˈzurɛk dɔmbrɔfˈskʲɛɡɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish"); lit. 'Dąbrowski's [Mazurka](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka")')[[b]](#cite_note-2) and formerly the "**Song of the Polish Legions in Italy**",[[c]](#cite_note-3) is the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland").[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-4)[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Trochimczyk2000-6)
The original lyrics were written by [Józef Wybicki](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Wybicki "Józef Wybicki") in [Reggio Emilia](/wiki/Reggio_Emilia "Reggio Emilia"), in [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), between 16 and 19 July 1797, two years after the [Third Partition of Poland](/wiki/Third_Partition_of_Poland "Third Partition of Poland") marked the end of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"). Its initial purpose was to raise the morale of [Jan Henryk Dąbrowski](/wiki/Jan_Henryk_D%C4%85browski "Jan Henryk Dąbrowski")'s [Polish Legions](/wiki/Polish_Legions_(Napoleonic_era) "Polish Legions (Napoleonic era)") that served with [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") in the [Italian campaigns of the French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/Italian_campaigns_of_the_French_Revolutionary_Wars "Italian campaigns of the French Revolutionary Wars"). The song expressed the idea that the nation of Poland, despite lacking an [independent](/wiki/Independence "Independence") state of their own, had not disappeared as long as the Polish people endured and fought in its name.[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Trochimczyk2000-6)

Following the declaration of independence of the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") in 1918, the song became its *de facto* national anthem, and was officially adopted in 1927.[[3]](#cite_note-Trochimczyk2000-6) It also inspired similar songs by other peoples struggling for independence during the 19th century, such as the Ukrainian anthem "[Ukraine Is Not Yet Perished](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Ukraine "National anthem of Ukraine")", the Israeli anthem "[Hatikvah (Our hope is not yet lost)](/wiki/Hatikvah "Hatikvah")", the Croatian reveille "[Croatia has not yet fallen](/wiki/Jo%C5%A1_Hrvatska_ni_propala "Još Hrvatska ni propala")" and the [Yugoslav](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") and [Slovak](/wiki/First_Slovak_Republic "First Slovak Republic") anthem "[Hey, Slavs](/wiki/Hey,_Slavs "Hey, Slavs")".[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Piesn_Legionow_-_Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zginela_%2876123473%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Piesn_Legionow_-_Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zginela_%2876123473%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piesn_Legionow_-_Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zginela_(76123473)_(cropped).jpg)

One of a series of postcards, designed by [Juliusz Kossak](/wiki/Juliusz_Kossak "Juliusz Kossak"), illustrating the lyrics of "Mazurek Dąbrowskiego"

It is also known by its original title, "Pieśń Legionów Polskich we Włoszech" ('Song of the [Polish Legions](/wiki/Polish_Legions_(Napoleonic_era) "Polish Legions (Napoleonic era)") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy")').[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Trochimczyk2000-6) As there are no official translations of the name into English, various translations of its Polish [incipit](/wiki/Incipit "Incipit") "Jeszcze Polska nie zginęła" include 'Poland has not yet perished',[[1]](#cite_note-CIA-4) 'Poland has not perished yet',[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5) 'Poland is not lost',[[4]](#cite_note-EDVR-Matrix-BVE-40866-7) 'Poland is not lost yet',[[5]](#cite_note-Jonson1905-8) 'Poland is not yet lost',[[6]](#cite_note-Soboleski1883-9) and 'Poland has not yet succumbed'.[[7]](#cite_note-polish-flag-10)

## Lyrics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Lyrics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Mazurek_D%C4%85browskiego_manuscript.jpg/250px-Mazurek_D%C4%85browskiego_manuscript.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mazurek_D%C4%85browskiego_manuscript.jpg)

Facsimile of [Wybicki's](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Wybicki "Józef Wybicki") manuscript of the "Song of the Polish Legions in Italy"

The original lyrics, authored by Wybicki, are a poem consisting of six [quatrains](/wiki/Quatrain "Quatrain") and a [refrain](/wiki/Refrain "Refrain") quatrain repeated after all but the last [stanza](/wiki/Stanza "Stanza"), all following an ABAB [rhyme scheme](/wiki/Rhyme_scheme "Rhyme scheme"). The official lyrics, based on a variant from 1806,[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11) "Poland has not yet died", suggesting a more violent cause of the nation's possible death.[[9]](#cite_note-davies-12) Wybicki's original manuscript was in the hands of his descendants until February 1944, when it was [lost](/wiki/Lost_literary_work "Lost literary work") in Wybicki's great-great-grandson, Johann von Roznowski's home in [Charlottenburg](/wiki/Charlottenburg "Charlottenburg") during the [Allied bombing of Berlin](/wiki/Battle_of_Berlin_(air) "Battle of Berlin (air)"). The manuscript is known today only from [facsimile](/wiki/Facsimile "Facsimile") copies, twenty-four of which were made in 1886 by Edward Rożnowski, Wybicki's grandson, who donated them to Polish libraries.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

The main theme of the poem is the idea that was novel in the times of early [nationalisms](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") based on centralized [nation-states](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") – that the lack of political sovereignty does not preclude the existence of a nation. As [Adam Mickiewicz](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz "Adam Mickiewicz") explained in 1842 to students of Slavic Literature in Paris, the song "The famous song of the Polish legions begins with lines that express the new history: Poland has not perished yet as long as we live. These words mean that people who have in them what constitutes the essence of a nation can prolong the existence of their country regardless of its political circumstances and may even strive to make it real again..."[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5) The song also includes a call to arms and expresses the hope that, under General Dąbrowski's command, the legionaries would rejoin their nation and retrieve "what the alien force has seized" through armed struggle.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Piesn_Legionow_-_Przejdziem_Wisle%2C_przejdziem_Warte_%2876123664%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Piesn_Legionow_-_Przejdziem_Wisle%2C_przejdziem_Warte_%2876123664%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piesn_Legionow_-_Przejdziem_Wisle,_przejdziem_Warte_(76123664)_(cropped).jpg)

*[Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") has shown us ways to victory*

The chorus and subsequent stanzas include heart-lifting examples of military heroes, set as role models for Polish soldiers: [Jan Henryk Dąbrowski](/wiki/Jan_Henryk_D%C4%85browski "Jan Henryk Dąbrowski"), Napoleon, [Stefan Czarniecki](/wiki/Stefan_Czarniecki "Stefan Czarniecki"), and [Tadeusz Kościuszko](/wiki/Tadeusz_Ko%C5%9Bciuszko "Tadeusz Kościuszko").

Dąbrowski, for whom the anthem is named, was a commander in the failed 1794 [Kościuszko Uprising](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Uprising "Kościuszko Uprising") against Russia. After the Third Partition in 1795, he came to Paris to seek French aid in re-establishing Polish independence and in 1796, he started the formation of the Polish Legions, a Polish unit of the French Revolutionary Army.

Bonaparte was, at the time when the song was written, a commander of the Italian campaign of [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and Dąbrowski's superior. Having already proven his skills as a military leader, he is described in the lyrics as the one "who has shown us ways to victory." Bonaparte is the only non-Polish person mentioned by name in the Polish anthem.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Piesn_Legionow_-_Jak_Czarniecki_do_Poznania_%2876123475%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Piesn_Legionow_-_Jak_Czarniecki_do_Poznania_%2876123475%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piesn_Legionow_-_Jak_Czarniecki_do_Poznania_(76123475)_(cropped).jpg)

*Like [Czarniecki](/wiki/Stefan_Czarniecki "Stefan Czarniecki") to [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań")...*

Stefan Czarniecki was a 17th-century [hetman](/wiki/Hetman "Hetman"), famous for his role in driving the [Swedish Army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army") out of Poland after an occupation that had left the country in ruins and is remembered by Poles as the [Deluge](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)"). With the outbreak of a [Dano-Swedish War](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_(1657%E2%80%9358) "Dano-Swedish War (1657–58)"), he continued his fight against Sweden in Denmark, from where he "returned across the sea" to fight the invaders alongside the king, who was then at the [Royal Castle in Poznań](/wiki/Royal_Castle_in_Pozna%C5%84 "Royal Castle in Poznań"). In the same castle, Józef Wybicki started his career as a lawyer in 1765.

Kościuszko, mentioned in a stanza now missing from the anthem, became a hero of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") before coming back to Poland to defend his native country from Russia in the [war of 1792](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Russian_War_of_1792 "Polish–Russian War of 1792") and a [national uprising he led](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Uprising "Kościuszko Uprising") in 1794. One of his major victories during the uprising was the [Battle of Racławice](/wiki/Battle_of_Rac%C5%82awice "Battle of Racławice"), where the result was partly due to Polish peasants armed with [scythes](/wiki/War_scythe "War scythe"). Alongside the scythes, the song mentioned other types of weaponry, traditionally used by the Polish *[szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")*, or nobility: the [sabre](/wiki/Sabre "Sabre"), known in Polish as *[szabla](/wiki/Szabla "Szabla")*, and the [backsword](/wiki/Backsword "Backsword").

Basia (a [feminine](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") of [Barbara](/wiki/Barbara_(given_name) "Barbara (given name)")) and her father are fictional characters. They are used to represent the women and elderly men who waited for the Polish soldiers to return home and liberate their fatherland. The route that Dąbrowski and his legions hoped to follow upon leaving Italy is hinted at by the words "we'll cross the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula"), we'll cross the [Warta](/wiki/Warta "Warta")", two major rivers flowing through the parts of Poland that were in [Austrian](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") and [Prussian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") hands at the time.

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Music")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Mazurek Dąbrowskiego"](/wiki/File:Mazurek_Dabrowskiego.ogg "File:Mazurek Dabrowskiego.ogg")

One verse of "Mazurek Dąbrowskiego" performed by the [U.S. Navy Band](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Band "United States Navy Band")

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

The melody of the Polish anthem is a lively and rhythmical [mazurka](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka"). Mazurka as a musical form derives from the stylization of traditional melodies for the folk dances of [Mazovia](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia"), a region in central Poland. It is characterized by a [triple meter](/wiki/Triple_meter "Triple meter") and strong accents placed irregularly on the second or third beat. Considered one of Poland's national dances in pre-partition times, it owes its popularity in 19th-century [Western European](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") [ballrooms](/wiki/Ballroom "Ballroom") to the [mazurkas](/wiki/List_of_compositions_by_Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin#Mazurkas "List of compositions by Frédéric Chopin") of [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin").[[11]](#cite_note-15)

The composer of "Mazurek Dąbrowskiego" is not known, though most contemporary performances of it utilize a modern arrangement by composer [Kazimierz Sikorski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Sikorski "Kazimierz Sikorski"). The melody is most probably Wybicki's adaptation of a folk tune that had already been popular during the second half of the 18th century. The composition used to be erroneously attributed to [Michał Kleofas Ogiński](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kleofas_Ogi%C5%84ski "Michał Kleofas Ogiński"), who was known to have written a [march](/wiki/March_(music) "March (music)") for Dąbrowski's legions. Several historians confused Ogiński's "Marche pour les Légions polonaises" ('March for the Polish Legions') with Wybicki's mazurka, possibly due to the mazurka's chorus "March, march, Dąbrowski", until Ogiński's sheet music for the march was discovered in 1938 and proven to be a different piece of music than Poland's national anthem.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

The first composer to use the anthem for an artistic music piece is always stated to be [Karol Kurpiński](/wiki/Karol_Kurpi%C5%84ski "Karol Kurpiński"). In 1821, he composed his piano/organ [Fugue](/wiki/Fugue "Fugue") on "Jeszcze Polska nie zginęła" (it was published in 1821 in Warsaw; the first modern edition by Rostislaw Wygranienko was printed only in 2009).[[12]](#cite_note-16)[[13]](#cite_note-17) However, [Karol Lipiński](/wiki/Karol_Lipi%C5%84ski "Karol Lipiński") used it in an overture for his opera *[Kłótnia przez zakład](/wiki/K%C5%82%C3%B3tnia_przez_zak%C5%82ad "Kłótnia przez zakład")* composed and staged in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") c. 1812.[[14]](#cite_note-Puslowski2014-18)

[Wojciech Sowiński](/w/index.php?title=Wojciech_Sowi%C5%84ski&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wojciech Sowiński (page does not exist)") [[pl](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wojciech_Sowi%C5%84ski "pl:Wojciech Sowiński")] was the next who arranged "Mazurek Dąbrowskiego" for the piano. The arrangement, accompanied by the lyrics in Polish and French, was published 1829 in Paris.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11) German composers who were moved by the suffering of the [November Uprising](/wiki/November_Uprising "November Uprising") wove the mazurek into their works. Examples include Richard Wagner's [Polonia Overture](/wiki/Polonia_(Wagner) "Polonia (Wagner)") and [Albert Lortzing](/wiki/Albert_Lortzing "Albert Lortzing")'s *Der Pole und sein Kind*.

The current official musical score of the national anthem was arranged by [Kazimierz Sikorski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Sikorski "Kazimierz Sikorski") and published by the Polish [Ministry of Culture and National Heritage](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_and_National_Heritage_(Poland) "Ministry of Culture and National Heritage (Poland)"). Sikorski's harmonization allows for each vocal version to be performed either *[a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella")* or together with any of the instrumental versions. Some orchestra parts, marked in the score as *[ad libitum](/wiki/Ad_libitum "Ad libitum")*, may be left out or replaced by other instruments of equivalent musical scale.[[2]](#cite_note-MFAoP-5)

In 1908, [Ignacy Jan Paderewski](/wiki/Ignacy_Jan_Paderewski "Ignacy Jan Paderewski"), later to become the first Prime Minister of independent Poland, [quoted](/wiki/Musical_quotation "Musical quotation") the anthem in a disguised way in his [Symphony in B minor "Polonia"](/wiki/Symphony_in_B_minor_(Paderewski) "Symphony in B minor (Paderewski)"). He scored it in [duple meter](/wiki/Duple_meter "Duple meter") rather than its standard triple meter.

The anthem was quoted by [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar") in his symphonic prelude *[Polonia](/wiki/Polonia_(Elgar) "Polonia (Elgar)")*, composed in 1915.

## Regulations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Regulations")]

The national anthem is, along with the [national coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Poland "Coat of arms of Poland") and the [national colors](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland "Flag of Poland"), one of three national symbols defined by the [Polish constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Constitution of the Republic of Poland").[[15]](#cite_note-const-19)
As such, it is protected by law which declares that treating the national symbols "with reverence and respect" is the "right and obligation" of every Polish citizen and all state organs, institutions and organizations.[[10]](#cite_note-act-13) The anthem should be performed or reproduced especially at celebrations of national holidays and anniversaries. Civilians should pay respect to the anthem by standing in a dignified manner; additionally, men should uncover their heads. Members of uniformed services should stand at attention; if their uniform includes headgear and they are not standing in an organized group, they should also perform the [two-finger salute](/wiki/Two-finger_salute "Two-finger salute"). The song is required to be played in the key of F major if played for a public purpose.[[16]](#cite_note-20) [Color guards](/wiki/Color_guard "Color guard") pay respect to the anthem by dipping their banners.[[10]](#cite_note-act-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Origin")]

See also: [Polish Legions (Napoleonic era)](/wiki/Polish_Legions_(Napoleonic_era) "Polish Legions (Napoleonic era)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Jan_Henryk_Dabrowski_2.jpg/250px-Jan_Henryk_Dabrowski_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jan_Henryk_Dabrowski_2.jpg)

[Jan Henryk Dąbrowski](/wiki/Jan_Henryk_D%C4%85browski "Jan Henryk Dąbrowski") (1755–1818)

In 1795, after a prolonged decline and despite last-minute attempts at constitutional reforms and armed resistance, the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") was ultimately [partitioned](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") by its three neighbors: [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") and [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). A once-vast and powerful empire was effectively erased from the map while monarchs of the partitioning powers pledged never to use the name "Poland" in their official titles. For many, including even leading representatives of the [Polish Enlightenment](/wiki/Polish_Enlightenment "Polish Enlightenment"), this new political situation meant the end of the Polish nation.[[17]](#cite_note-czaplin-21) In the words of [Hugo Kołłątaj](/wiki/Hugo_Ko%C5%82%C5%82%C4%85taj "Hugo Kołłątaj"), a notable Polish political thinker of the time, "Poland no longer belonged to currently extant nations,"[[e]](#cite_note-22) while historian [Tadeusz Czacki](/wiki/Tadeusz_Czacki "Tadeusz Czacki") declared that Poland "was now effaced from the number of nations."[[f]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Jozef_Wybicki_1805_%2814441287%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jozef_Wybicki_1805_%2814441287%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jozef_Wybicki_1805_(14441287)_(cropped).jpg)

[Józef Wybicki](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Wybicki "Józef Wybicki") (1747–1822)

Meanwhile, Polish patriots and revolutionaries turned for help to [France](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), Poland's traditional ally, which was at war with Austria (member of the [First Coalition](/wiki/First_Coalition "First Coalition")) at the time. [Józef Wybicki](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Wybicki "Józef Wybicki") was among the leading moderate émigré politicians seeking French aid in re-establishing Polish independence. In 1796, he came up with the idea of creating Polish Legions within the [French Revolutionary Army](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Army "French Revolutionary Army"). To this end, he convinced General [Jan Henryk Dąbrowski](/wiki/Jan_Henryk_D%C4%85browski "Jan Henryk Dąbrowski"), a hero of the [Greater Poland campaign](/wiki/Greater_Poland_Uprising_(1794) "Greater Poland Uprising (1794)") of the 1794 [Kościuszko Uprising](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Uprising "Kościuszko Uprising"), to come to Paris and present the plan to the [French Directory](/wiki/French_Directory "French Directory"). Dąbrowski was sent by the Directory to Napoleon, who was then spreading the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") in northern Italy. In January 1797, the newly created French-controlled [Cisalpine Republic](/wiki/Cisalpine_Republic "Cisalpine Republic") accepted Dąbrowski's offer and a Polish legion was formed. Dąbrowski and his soldiers hoped to fight against Austria under Napoleon and, subsequently, march across the Austrian territory, "from Italy to Poland", where they would ignite a national uprising.[[17]](#cite_note-czaplin-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Lapide_Wybicki.jpg/250px-Lapide_Wybicki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lapide_Wybicki.jpg)

A commemorative plaque in [Reggio Emilia](/wiki/Reggio_Emilia "Reggio Emilia"), Italy

In early July 1797, Wybicki arrived in Reggio Emilia where the Polish Legions were then quartered and where he wrote the *Song of the Polish Legions* soon afterwards. He first sung it at a private meeting of Polish officers in the Legions' headquarters at the episcopal palace in Reggio. The first public performance most probably took place on 16 July 1797 during a military parade in Reggio's Piazza del Duomo (Cathedral Square). On 20 July, it was played again as the Legions were marching off from Reggio to [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), the Cisalpine capital.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

With its heart-lifting lyrics and folk melody, the song soon became a popular tune among Polish legionaries. On 29 August 1797, Dąbrowski already wrote to Wybicki from [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"): "soldiers gain more and more taste for your song."[[g]](#cite_note-24) It appealed to both officers, usually émigré noblemen, and simple soldiers, most of whom were [Galician](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)") peasants who had been drafted into the Austrian army and captured as [POWs](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") by the French. The last stanza, referring to Kościuszko, who famously fought for freedom of the entire nation rather than the nobility alone, and the "scythes of Racławice", seems to be directed particularly at the latter. Wybicki may have even hoped for Kościuszko to arrive in Italy and personally lead the Legions, which might explain why the chorus "March, march, Dąbrowski" is not repeated after the last stanza. At that time, Wybicki was not yet aware that Kościuszko had already returned to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

### Rising popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rising popularity")]

The song became popular in Poland as early as late 1797 and quickly became an object of variations and modifications. A variant from 1798 introduced some stylistic changes, which have since become standard, such as replacing *nie umarła* ('not dead') with *nie zginęła* ('not perished') or *do Polski z ziemi włoski* ('to Poland from the Italian land') with *z ziemi włoskiej do Polski* ('from the Italian land to Poland'). It also added four new stanzas, now forgotten, written from the viewpoint of Polish patriots waiting for General Dąbrowski to bring freedom and human rights to Poland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Piesn_Legionow_-_Mowi_ojciec_do_swej_Basi_%2876123674%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Piesn_Legionow_-_Mowi_ojciec_do_swej_Basi_%2876123674%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piesn_Legionow_-_Mowi_ojciec_do_swej_Basi_(76123674)_(cropped).jpg)

*Father, in tears, says to his Basia...*

The ultimate fate of the Polish Legions in Italy was different from that promised by Wybicki's song. Rather than coming back to Poland, they were exploited by the French government to quell uprisings in Italy, Germany and, later, in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") where they were decimated by [war](/wiki/Haitian_Revolution "Haitian Revolution") and disease.[[17]](#cite_note-czaplin-21) Polish national hopes were revived with the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War (part of the [War of the Fourth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fourth_Coalition "War of the Fourth Coalition")) in 1806. Napoleon called Dąbrowski and Wybicki to come back from Italy and help gather support for the French army in Polish-populated parts of Prussia. On 6 November 1806, both generals arrived in [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"),[[17]](#cite_note-czaplin-21) enthusiastically greeted by locals singing "Poland Is Not Yet Lost".[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11) The ensuing [Greater Poland Uprising](/wiki/Greater_Poland_Uprising_(1806) "Greater Poland Uprising (1806)") and Napoleon's victory over Russian forces at [Friedland](/wiki/Battle_of_Friedland "Battle of Friedland") led to the creation of a French-controlled Polish puppet state known as the [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw").[[17]](#cite_note-czaplin-21)

"Poland Is Not Yet Lost" was one of the most popular patriotic songs in the duchy, stopping short of becoming that entity's national anthem. Among other occasions, it was sung in Warsaw on 16 June 1807 to celebrate the battle of Friedland, in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") as it was liberated by Prince [Józef Poniatowski](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Poniatowski "Józef Poniatowski") on 19 July 1809, and at a ball in Warsaw on 23 December 1809, the birthday of [Frederick Augustus](/wiki/Frederick_Augustus_I_of_Saxony "Frederick Augustus I of Saxony"), King of Saxony and Duke of Warsaw. On the occasion of Dąbrowski's [name day](/wiki/Name_day "Name day") on 25 December 1810 in Poznań, Dąbrowski and Wybicki led the mazurka to the tune of "Poland Is Not Yet Lost". Although the melody of Wybicki's song remained unchanged and widely known, the lyrics kept changing. With the signing of a Franco-Russian alliance at [Tilsit](/wiki/Treaties_of_Tilsit "Treaties of Tilsit") in 1807, the fourth stanza, specifically mentioning Russians as Poland's enemies, was removed. The last stanza, referring to Kościuszko, who had grown suspicious of Napoleon and refused to lend his support to the emperor's war in Poland, met the same fate.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

> The blow struck with such skill, with such force unsurpassed,  
> That the strings rang out boldly, like trumpets of brass,  
> And from them to the heavens that song wafted, cherished,  
> That triumphal march: Poland has never yet perished!  
> ...March Dąbrowski to Poland! – The audience entire  
> Clapped, and all "March Dąbrowski!" cried out as a choir.

Adam Mickiewicz,   
*[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* (Book Twelve, *Love and Friendship!*)[[18]](#cite_note-25)

The anthem is mentioned twice in *[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")*, the Polish [national epic](/wiki/National_epic "National epic") written by Adam Mickiewicz in 1834, but set in the years 1811–1812. The author makes the first reference to the song when Tadeusz, the main protagonist, returns home and, recalling childhood memories, pulls the string of a chiming clock to hear the "old Dąbrowski's Mazurka" once again. [Music boxes](/wiki/Music_box "Music box") and [musical clocks](/wiki/Musical_clock "Musical clock") playing the melody of *Poland Is Not Yet Lost* belonged to popular patriotic paraphernalia of that time. The song appears in the epic poem again when Jankiel, a Jewish [dulcimerist](/wiki/Hammered_dulcimer "Hammered dulcimer") and ardent Polish patriot, plays the mazurka in the presence of General Dąbrowski himself.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zgin%C4%99%C5%82a.jpg/250px-Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zgin%C4%99%C5%82a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeszcze_Polska_nie_zgin%C4%99%C5%82a.jpg)

Charles Michel Guilbert d'Anelle, *Expiring Soldier of Liberty* (1849). The painting shows a dying [freedom fighter](/wiki/Freedom_fighter "Freedom fighter") scrawling "Poland is not yet lost" in his blood.

With Napoleon's defeat and the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815 came a century of foreign domination over Poland interspersed with occasional bursts of armed rebellion. "Poland Is Not Yet Lost" continued to be sung throughout that period, especially during national uprisings. During the [November Uprising](/wiki/November_Uprising "November Uprising") against Russia in 1830–1831, the song was chanted in the battlefields of [Stoczek](/wiki/Battle_of_Stoczek "Battle of Stoczek"), [Olszynka Grochowska](/wiki/Battle_of_Olszynka_Grochowska "Battle of Olszynka Grochowska"), and [Iganie](/wiki/Battle_of_Iganie "Battle of Iganie"). In peacetime, Polish patriots performed it at homes, official functions and political demonstrations. New variants of the song, of various artistic value and length of life, abounded. At least 16 alternative versions were penned during the November Uprising alone. At times, Dąbrowski's name was replaced by other national heroes: from [Józef Chłopicki](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Ch%C5%82opicki "Józef Chłopicki") during the November Uprising to [Józef Piłsudski](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Pi%C5%82sudski "Józef Piłsudski") during the First World War to [Władysław Sikorski](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Sikorski "Władysław Sikorski") during the Second World War. New lyrics were also written in regional dialects of Polish, from [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") to [Ermland](/wiki/Warmia "Warmia") and [Masuria](/wiki/Masuria "Masuria").[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11) A variant known as "Marsz Polonii" ('March of Polonia') spread among [Polish immigrants](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").

Mass political emigration following the defeat of the November Uprising, known as the [Great Emigration](/wiki/Great_Emigration "Great Emigration"), brought "Poland Is Not Yet Lost" to Western Europe. It soon found favor from Britain to France to Germany, where it was performed as a token of sympathy with the Polish cause. It was also highly esteemed in Central Europe, where various, mostly [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples"), peoples struggling for their own independence, looked to the Polish anthem for inspiration. Back in Poland, however, especially in the parts under Russian and Prussian rule, it was becoming increasingly risky to sing the anthem in public. Polish patriotic songs were banned in Prussia in 1850; between 1873 and 1911, German courts passed 44 sentences for singing such songs, 20 of which were specifically for singing "Poland Is Not Yet Lost". When Poland was part of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), public performance of the song often ended with police intervention.[[8]](#cite_note-kuczyn-11)

### Choice of national anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Choice of national anthem")]

When Poland re-emerged as an independent state after World War I in 1918, it had to decide on its national symbols. While the coat of arms and the flag were officially adopted as early as 1919, the question of a national anthem had to wait. Apart from "Poland Is Not Yet Lost", there were other popular patriotic songs that could compete for the status of an official national anthem.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Bogurodzica_rekopis1407.png/250px-Bogurodzica_rekopis1407.png)](/wiki/File:Bogurodzica_rekopis1407.png)

Sheet music for *[Bogurodzica](/wiki/Bogurodzica "Bogurodzica")* from 1407

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Poland_is_Not_Yet_Lost%21.png/250px-Poland_is_Not_Yet_Lost%21.png)](/wiki/File:Poland_is_Not_Yet_Lost!.png)

Poland is Not Yet Lost!

In the Middle Ages, the role of a national anthem was played by hymns. Among them were "[Bogurodzica](/wiki/Bogurodzica "Bogurodzica")" ('Mother of God'), one of the oldest (11th–12th century) known literary texts in Polish, and the Latin "[Gaude Mater Polonia](/wiki/Gaude_Mater_Polonia "Gaude Mater Polonia")" ('Rejoice, Mother Poland'), written in the 13th century to celebrate the canonization of Bishop [Stanislaus of Szczepanów](/wiki/Stanislaus_of_Szczepan%C3%B3w "Stanislaus of Szczepanów"), the patron saint of Poland. Both were chanted on special occasions and on battlefields. The latter is sung nowadays at university ceremonies. During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland") and the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), several songs, both religious and secular, were written with the specific purpose of creating a new national anthem. Examples include the 16th-century Latin prayer *Oratio pro Republica et Rege* ('Prayer for the Commonwealth and the King') by a Calvinist poet, [Andrzej Trzeciński](/w/index.php?title=Andrzej_Trzeci%C5%84ski&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andrzej Trzeciński (page does not exist)"), and "Hymn do miłości Ojczyzny" ('Hymn to the Love of the Fatherland') written in 1744 by Prince-Bishop [Ignacy Krasicki](/wiki/Ignacy_Krasicki "Ignacy Krasicki"). They failed, however, to win substantial favor with the populace. Another candidate was "[Bóg się rodzi](/wiki/B%C3%B3g_si%C4%99_rodzi "Bóg się rodzi")" ('God Is Born'), whose melody was originally a 16th-century coronation [polonaise (dance)](/wiki/Polonaise_(dance) "Polonaise (dance)") for Polish kings.

The official anthem of the Russian-controlled [Congress Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland") was "Pieśń narodowa na pomyślność Króla" (lit. 'National Song to the King's Prosperity') written in 1816 by [Alojzy Feliński](/wiki/Alojzy_Feli%C5%84ski "Alojzy Feliński") and Jan Kaszewski. Initially unpopular, it evolved in the early 1860s into an important religious and patriotic hymn. The final verse, which originally begged "Save, O Lord, our King", was substituted with "Return us, O Lord, our free Fatherland" while the melody was replaced with that of a Marian hymn. The result, known today as "[Boże, coś Polskę](/wiki/Bo%C5%BCe,_co%C5%9B_Polsk%C4%99 "Boże, coś Polskę")" (from the first lines "Boże! Coś Polskę przez tak liczne wieki / Otaczał blaskiem potęgi i chwały...", "Lord! Who for so many ages enclosed Poland with the light of power and glory..."), has been sung in Polish churches ever since, with the final verse alternating between "Return..." and "Bless, O Lord, our free Fatherland", depending on Poland's political situation.

A national song that was particularly popular during the November Uprising was "[Warszawianka](/wiki/Warszawianka_(1831) "Warszawianka (1831)")", originally written in French as "La Varsovienne" by [Casimir Delavigne](/wiki/Casimir_Delavigne "Casimir Delavigne"), with melody by [Karol Kurpiński](/wiki/Karol_Kurpi%C5%84ski "Karol Kurpiński"). The song praised Polish insurgents taking their ideals from the French [July Revolution](/wiki/July_Revolution "July Revolution") of 1830. A peasant rebellion against Polish nobles, which took place in western [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") in 1846 and was encouraged by Austrian authorities who wished to thwart a new uprising attempt, moved [Kornel Ujejski](/wiki/Kornel_Ujejski "Kornel Ujejski") to write a mournful [chorale](/wiki/Chorale "Chorale") entitled "Z dymem pożarów" ('With the Smoke of Fires'). With the music composed by [Józef Nikorowicz](/w/index.php?title=J%C3%B3zef_Nikorowicz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Józef Nikorowicz (page does not exist)") [[pl](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Nikorowicz "pl:Józef Nikorowicz")], it became one of the most popular national songs of the time, although it declined into obscurity during the 20th century. In 1908, [Maria Konopnicka](/wiki/Maria_Konopnicka "Maria Konopnicka") and [Feliks Nowowiejski](/wiki/Feliks_Nowowiejski "Feliks Nowowiejski") created "[Rota](/wiki/Rota_(poem) "Rota (poem)")" ('Oath'), a song protesting against the oppression of the Polish population of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), who were subject to eviction from their land and forced assimilation. First publicly performed in 1910, during a quincentennial celebration of the Polish–Lithuanian victory over the [Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Knights "Teutonic Knights") at [Grunwald](/wiki/Battle_of_Grunwald "Battle of Grunwald"), it too became one of the most treasured national Polish songs.

At the inauguration of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1945, no delegation from Poland had been invited.[[19]](#cite_note-YoUN-26): 12[[h]](#cite_note-CoUN-31) The Polish pianist [Artur Rubinstein](/wiki/Artur_Rubinstein "Artur Rubinstein"), who was to perform the opening concert at the inauguration, began the concert by stating his deep disappointment that the conference did not have a delegation from Poland. Rubinstein later described becoming overwhelmed by a blind fury and angrily pointing out to the public the absence of the [Polish flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland "Flag of Poland"). He then sat down to the piano and played "Poland Is Not Yet Lost" loudly and slowly, repeating the final part in a great thunderous *forte*. When he had finished, the public rose to their feet and gave him a great ovation.[[24]](#cite_note-32)

Over 60 years later, on 22 September 2005, [Aleksander Kwaśniewski](/wiki/Aleksander_Kwa%C5%9Bniewski "Aleksander Kwaśniewski"), [President of Poland](/wiki/President_of_Poland "President of Poland"), said:

> For the [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") is rightly criticised for being anachronistic, for reflecting the old world that is drifting away into the past. Particularly we, the Polish people, and all the nations of Central and Eastern Europe find it difficult to forget about [that](/wiki/Western_betrayal "Western betrayal"). The UN idea dates back to 1943; to the [meeting of the "Big Three" in Tehran](/wiki/Tehran_Conference "Tehran Conference"); to the illusions that [Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") harboured about [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), benevolently nicknamed "Uncle Joe". As a result, the road to [San Francisco](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization") led via [Yalta](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference"). And even though [Poland had made a major contribution](/wiki/Polish_contribution_to_World_War_II "Polish contribution to World War II") to the victory which put an end to the Second World War, in June 1945 a representative of our country was not allowed to put his signature to the [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter").[[h]](#cite_note-CoUN-31) We remember that event when Artur Rubinstein, seeing that there was no Polish delegation at the concert to mark the signing of the Charter, decided to play the Dąbrowski Mazurka, Poland's national anthem, to demonstrate that "Poland was not lost yet", that Poland lived on. I am recalling this because I had a very touching moment a few days ago in the same San Francisco opera house, to which I was invited for the opening of the season. This time it was the orchestra that played the Dąbrowski Mazurka, and at that moment the memories of the great Artur Rubinstein and his performance came back with full force and it was very touching indeed for me. The UN is rooted in the Second World War and in the post-war situation; it reflects the balance of power of that era.[[25]](#cite_note-33)

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poland_Is_Not_Yet_Lost&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Influence")]

During the European [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"), "Poland Is Not Yet Lost" won favor throughout Europe as a revolutionary anthem. This led the [Slovak](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") poet [Samo Tomášik](/wiki/Samo_Tom%C3%A1%C5%A1ik "Samo Tomášik") to write the ethnic anthem, "[Hej, Sloveni](/wiki/Hej,_Sloveni "Hej, Sloveni")", based on the slowed melody of the Polish national anthem. It was later adopted by the [Prague Slavic Congress](/wiki/Prague_Slavic_Congress,_1848 "Prague Slavic Congress, 1848") as the Pan-Slavic Anthem. During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), a translation of this anthem became the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), and later, [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro"). The similarity of the anthems sometimes confused these countries' football or volleyball matches. However, after the 2006 split between the two, neither [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") nor [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") kept the song as its national anthem, instead choosing "[Bože pravde](/wiki/Bo%C5%BEe_pravde "Bože pravde")" and "[Oj, svijetla majska zoro](/wiki/Oj,_svijetla_majska_zoro "Oj, svijetla majska zoro")" respectively. The Polish national anthem is also notable for influencing the lyrics of the [national anthem of Ukraine](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Ukraine "National anthem of Ukraine").[[26]](#cite_note-34)
The anthem is played on [First Programme of Polish Radio](/w/index.php?title=Program_Pierwszy_Polskiego_Radia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Program Pierwszy Polskiego Radia (page does not exist)") every day at midnight.

The line *Poland is not yet lost* has become proverbial in some languages. For example, in [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), *[noch ist Polen nicht verloren](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/noch_ist_Polen_nicht_verloren "wikt:noch ist Polen nicht verloren")* is a common saying meaning all is not lost'.[[27]](#cite_note-35) In [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), the similar phrase *[än är inte Polen förlorat](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C3%A4n_%C3%A4r_inte_Polen_f%C3%B6rlorat "wikt:än är inte Polen förlorat")* is also used in the same context.

Additionally, the Italian anthem "[Il Canto degli Italiani](/wiki/Il_Canto_degli_Italiani "Il Canto degli Italiani")" contains a reference to the [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") by [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") and [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), due to [the two countries' close relations](/wiki/Italy%E2%80%93Poland_relations "Italy–Poland relations").
